questionText: The annual rainfall, $X$ mm, at a weather station is modelled by $X \sim N(720, 60^2)$. The wettest $5\%$ of years have annual rainfall exceeding a value $a$ mm. Find $a$, giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the condition
steps.explanation: Rainfall is normal with mean $\mu = 720$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 60$. The wettest $5\%$ of years exceed $a$, so $P(X > a) = 0.05$.
The inverse-normal reads from the area to the left, so we rewrite: if $5\%$ of years are above $a$, then $95\%$ are below it, giving $P(X < a) = 0.95$.
The standardised value with $95\%$ of the area to its left is $z = 1.645$. It is positive because a value only $5\%$ of years exceed must lie well above the mean.
Standardising the unknown rainfall $a$ must give this $z$-value, so we equate the two.
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma$ and adding $\mu$ makes $a$ the subject — the general rule for turning a $z$-value back into a rainfall figure.
Putting in the mean $720$, the standard deviation $60$ and the $z$-value $1.645$ gives the rainfall in millimetres.
The arithmetic gives a rainfall of about $818.7$ mm.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: Rounding gives the rainfall exceeded in only the wettest $5\%$ of years.
The value sits well above the mean of $720$ mm, as it should: only an unusually wet year — the top $5\%$ — exceeds it.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $a \approx 819$ mm (3 s.f.) — the rainfall exceeded by the wettest $5\%$ of years.
